seo.title: Building Developer Tools with open-source tools
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for Developer Tools with open-source tools.

content.intro: This guide outlines the technical implementation of a production-grade CLI tool using TypeScript and Commander.js. It focuses on creating a robust architecture that supports cross-platform execution, automated configuration management, and developer-centric error reporting.
prerequisites: Node.js (v18 or higher) and npm/pnpm installed
Basic proficiency with TypeScript and asynchronous programming

steps.title: Initialize Project with ESM and Shebang Support
steps.explanation: Create a new project directory and initialize it as an ES Module. This ensures compatibility with modern libraries. You must include a shebang line at the top of your entry point to tell the OS loader which interpreter to use.
pitfalls: Forgetting the shebang line will cause the script to fail when executed as a binary.
Ensure 'type': 'module' is set in package.json to avoid ESM/CJS interop issues.

steps.title: Implement Robust Configuration Persistence
steps.explanation: Use the 'conf' library to manage user preferences, API tokens, and local state. Store these in the standard OS-specific config directories (e.g., ~/.config or %APPDATA%) to ensure persistence across sessions.
pitfalls: Hardcoding configuration paths instead of using standard libraries leads to cross-platform failures.
Storing sensitive API keys in plain text without warning the user.

steps.title: Design Intuitive Subcommands and Options
steps.explanation: Structure your CLI into logical subcommands. Use Commander.js to define mandatory arguments and optional flags. Grouping logic into separate command files improves maintainability as the tool scales.
pitfalls: Using inconsistent naming conventions for flags (e.g., mixing camelCase and kebab-case).
Failing to provide helpful descriptions, which breaks the built-in --help output.

steps.title: Integrate Visual Feedback and TTY Detection
steps.explanation: Use 'ora' for spinners and 'chalk' for colored output. Crucially, detect if the process is running in a TTY (interactive terminal) to disable animations in CI/CD environments, preventing log pollution.
pitfalls: Running spinners in non-TTY environments (like GitHub Actions) results in hundreds of lines of escape codes.
Overusing colors can make the tool inaccessible to users with color blindness.

steps.title: Standardize Error Handling and Exit Codes
steps.explanation: Implement a global error handler that catches asynchronous exceptions. Map specific failure types to standard POSIX exit codes (e.g., 1 for general errors, 127 for command not found) to allow scriptability in bash/zsh.
pitfalls: Exiting with code 0 after a failure prevents CI/CD pipelines from stopping on errors.
Printing stack traces to the user instead of actionable, human-readable error messages.

steps.title: Configure Distribution and Binary Linking
steps.explanation: Map your entry point in the 'bin' field of package.json. Use a build step (like esbuild or tsc) to bundle the TypeScript into a single executable JS file, ensuring users don't need to install dev dependencies to run the tool.
pitfalls: Publishing the src/ directory instead of the compiled dist/ directory.
Missing 'chmod +x' on the output file, which prevents execution on Unix-based systems after installation.

content.conclusion: By following this structured approach, you have built a CLI tool that adheres to modern DevTool standards: ESM support, standardized configuration, environment-aware UI, and proper POSIX exit codes. This foundation allows for seamless integration into both developer workstations and automated pipelines.
